questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: how are traditional Linux read, write, and execute mode bits grouped?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Mode bits apply broadly to filesystem objects.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Mode bits apply broadly to filesystem objects.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Mode bits apply broadly to filesystem objects.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Execute permission on a directory controls traversal rather than running the directory.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Execute permission on a directory controls traversal rather than running the directory.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Execute permission on a directory controls traversal rather than running the directory.

answers.text: From what I've seen, they are grouped for the owning user, owning group, and others.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. They are grouped for the owning user, owning group, and others.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. They are grouped for the owning user, owning group, and others.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: what is a Linux process?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Each process has an identifier and a virtual address space.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Each process has an identifier and a virtual address space.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Each process has an identifier and a virtual address space.

answers.text: The way I understand it: a running program instance with execution state and resources.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A running program instance with execution state and resources.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A running program instance with execution state and resources.

answers.text: Short version: A file that can never have more than one running instance.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The same executable can be used by many processes.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The same executable can be used by many processes.

questions.prompt: Curious where people land on this: what does a shell pipeline such as `a | b` connect?

answers.text: My answer: The standard output of `a` to the standard input of `b`.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The standard output of `a` to the standard input of `b`.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The standard output of `a` to the standard input of `b`.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: The filesystems of two machines automatically.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A shell pipe carries a byte stream between local processes unless other tools add networking.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A shell pipe carries a byte stream between local processes unless other tools add networking.

answers.text: The way I understand it: a pipe preserves application-level message or record boundaries.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A normal pipe is a byte stream; applications must define their own framing when needed.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A normal pipe is a byte stream; applications must define their own framing when needed.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what does `fork()` create?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: The child is a separate process with its own PID.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The child is a separate process with its own PID.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The child is a separate process with its own PID.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Copy-on-write commonly delays copying memory pages until one process writes.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Copy-on-write commonly delays copying memory pages until one process writes.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Copy-on-write commonly delays copying memory pages until one process writes.

answers.text: From what I've seen, a child process initially based on the calling process.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A child process initially based on the calling process.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A child process initially based on the calling process.

questions.prompt: Quick gut check: what is a Unix signal?

answers.text: I'd go with this: Some signals can be handled or blocked, while SIGKILL cannot be caught.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Some signals can be handled or blocked, while SIGKILL cannot be caught.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Some signals can be handled or blocked, while SIGKILL cannot be caught.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: an asynchronous notification delivered to a process or thread.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. An asynchronous notification delivered to a process or thread.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. An asynchronous notification delivered to a process or thread.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, a guaranteed queue that preserves unlimited repeated notifications.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Standard signals can coalesce and are not an unlimited message queue.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Standard signals can coalesce and are not an unlimited message queue.

questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: how does a symbolic link differ from a hard link?

answers.text: The practical answer is: a symbolic link stores a path to another filesystem object.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A symbolic link stores a path to another filesystem object.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A symbolic link stores a path to another filesystem object.

answers.text: My answer: A symbolic link must stay on the same filesystem as its target.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Symbolic links can point across filesystem boundaries.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Symbolic links can point across filesystem boundaries.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: The target must exist before a symbolic link can be created.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A symbolic link can be created for a nonexistent path and will then be dangling.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A symbolic link can be created for a nonexistent path and will then be dangling.

questions.prompt: Curious where people land on this: what does a hard link reference?

answers.text: My answer: A pathname that is followed dynamically each time.

feedback.legit: Not this one. That describes a symbolic link; a hard link is another name for the inode.
feedback.sus: Good catch. That describes a symbolic link; a hard link is another name for the inode.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Removing one hard link does not remove the data while another link remains.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Removing one hard link does not remove the data while another link remains.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Removing one hard link does not remove the data while another link remains.

answers.text: The way I understand it: the same inode and underlying file data as another directory entry.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The same inode and underlying file data as another directory entry.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The same inode and underlying file data as another directory entry.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: does Linux load average equal CPU utilization percentage?

answers.text: The way I understand it: the three values summarize different time windows.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The three values summarize different time windows.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The three values summarize different time windows.

answers.text: Short version: Yes, a load of 1.0 always means 100 percent CPU use on every machine.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Load average counts runnable and certain uninterruptible tasks and must be interpreted with CPU count.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Load average counts runnable and certain uninterruptible tasks and must be interpreted with CPU count.

answers.text: I'd go with this: I/O-waiting tasks can contribute to load average.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. I/O-waiting tasks can contribute to load average.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. I/O-waiting tasks can contribute to load average.

questions.prompt: Quick gut check: what do Linux control groups provide?

answers.text: I'd go with this: Resource accounting and limits for groups of processes.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Resource accounting and limits for groups of processes.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Resource accounting and limits for groups of processes.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: a replacement for all filesystem permissions.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Cgroups manage resources, while permissions remain a separate security control.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Cgroups manage resources, while permissions remain a separate security control.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, control groups give processes separate views of process IDs and network interfaces.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Namespaces isolate resource views; cgroups primarily account for and control resource usage.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Namespaces isolate resource views; cgroups primarily account for and control resource usage.

questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: what do Linux namespaces isolate?

answers.text: The practical answer is: they automatically encrypt all data between processes.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Namespaces isolate views; encryption is a separate feature.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Namespaces isolate views; encryption is a separate feature.

answers.text: My answer: Containers combine multiple namespace types with other controls.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Containers combine multiple namespace types with other controls.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Containers combine multiple namespace types with other controls.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Selected views of system resources such as process IDs, mounts, or networks.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Selected views of system resources such as process IDs, mounts, or networks.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Selected views of system resources such as process IDs, mounts, or networks.

questions.prompt: Forum check: why use epoll for many file descriptors?

answers.text: From what I've seen, edge-triggered use requires draining readiness carefully to avoid missed progress.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Edge-triggered use requires draining readiness carefully to avoid missed progress.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Edge-triggered use requires draining readiness carefully to avoid missed progress.

answers.text: The practical answer is: it can report readiness changes without scanning every descriptor on each wait.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It can report readiness changes without scanning every descriptor on each wait.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It can report readiness changes without scanning every descriptor on each wait.

answers.text: My answer: It turns blocking file operations into CPU-parallel work automatically.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Epoll reports readiness; the application still performs the I/O.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Epoll reports readiness; the application still performs the I/O.

questions.prompt: Settle a debate for me: what can happen when Linux cannot satisfy memory demand?

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: An out-of-memory event can occur only when swap is disabled.

feedback.legit: Not this one. OOM can occur with or without swap when the relevant memory domain cannot satisfy an allocation.
feedback.sus: Good catch. OOM can occur with or without swap when the relevant memory domain cannot satisfy an allocation.

answers.text: From what I've seen, the kernel always adds physical RAM automatically.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Memory is finite; reclaim, swap, and termination are possible responses.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Memory is finite; reclaim, swap, and termination are possible responses.

answers.text: The practical answer is: the OOM killer always terminates the process using the most memory.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The kernel uses an OOM scoring policy, so raw memory use alone does not determine the victim.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The kernel uses an OOM scoring policy, so raw memory use alone does not determine the victim.
